question: UC is planning a massive SF implementation with large volumes of data. As part of the org's implementation, several roles, territories, groups, and sharing rules have been configured. The data architect has been tasked with loading all of the required data, including user data, in a timely manner. What should a data architect do to minimize data load times due to system calculations?
optionA: Enable defer sharing calculations, and suspend sharing rule calculations
optionB: Load the data through data loader, and turn on parallel processing.
optionC: Leverage the Bulk API and concurrent processing with multiple batches
optionD: Enable granular locking to avoid "UNABLE_TO_LOCK_ROW" error.
explanation: Defer Sharing Calculations lets you suspend group-membership and sharing-rule recalculation during a large user/role/group load, then run one bulk recalculation afterward, which is far cheaper than recalculating per record. Bulk API/parallel processing (B, C) speed DML throughput but do nothing about the sharing-recalculation overhead that is the stated bottleneck; granular locking (D) addresses row-lock contention, not sharing calculations.

question: UC has millions of case records with case history and SLA data. UC's compliance team would like historical cases to be accessible for 10 years for Audit purpose. What solution should a data architect recommend?
optionA: Archive Case data using Salesforce Archiving process
optionB: Purchase more data storage to support case object
optionC: Use a custom object to store archived case data.
optionD: Use a custom big object to store archived case data.
explanation: Big objects store billions of records without counting against regular storage limits or degrading transactional performance, making them ideal for 10-year audit retention. A standard custom object (C) consumes storage and degrades report/query performance at millions of rows; buying storage (B) is costly and leaves data in the transactional store affecting performance.

question: A large telecommunication provider that provides internet services to both residence and business has the following attributes: A customer who purchases its services for their home will be created as an Account in Salesforce. Individuals within the same house address will be created as Contact in Salesforce. Businesses are created as Accounts in Salesforce. Some of the customers have both services at their home and business. What should a data architect recommend for a single view of these customers without creating multiple customer records?
optionA: Customers are created as Contacts and related to Business and Residential Accounts using the Account Contact Relationships.
optionB: Customers are created as Person Accounts and related to Business and Residential Accounts using the Account Contact relationship.
optionC: Customer are created as individual objects and relate with Accounts for Business and Residence accounts.
optionD: Customers are created as Accounts for Residence Account and use Parent Account to relate Business Account.
explanation: Account Contact Relationships (ACR) let a single Contact be associated with many Accounts, so one customer record can link to both a Residential and a Business Account, delivering one view without duplication. Person Accounts (B) would create a separate consumer record disconnected from the existing Contact model and cannot cleanly relate to a business Account; Parent Account hierarchy (D) models org structure, not a person spanning two accounts. Corrected from the source key (which contradicted its own explanation).

question: Universal Containers (UC) has over 10 million accounts with an average of 20 opportunities with each account. A Sales Executive at UC needs to generate a daily report for all opportunities in a specific opportunity stage. Which two key considerations should be made to make sure the performance of the report is not degraded due to large data volume?
optionA: Number of queries running at a time.
optionB: Number of joins used in report query.
optionC: Number of records returned by report query.
optionD: Number of characters in report query.
explanation: Each object a report touches adds a join, and joins across 10M accounts and 200M opportunities dominate cost, so limiting cross-object joins (B) keeps the query plan efficient. The volume of rows returned (C) drives processing/rendering time, so a selective indexed filter on stage matters. Concurrency (A) and query character count (D) are not meaningful drivers at LDV.

question: A customer is facing locking issued when importing large data volumes of order records that are children in a master-detail relationship with the Account object. What is the recommended way to avoid locking issues during import?
optionA: Import Account records first followed by order records after sorting order by OrderID.
optionB: Import Account records first followed by order records after sorting orders by AccountID.
optionC: Change the relationship to Lookup and update the relationship to master-detail after import.
optionD: Import Order records and Account records separately and populate AccountID in orders using batch Apex.
explanation: In a master-detail load, updating a child locks its parent; sorting child Orders by AccountID (B) groups all children of the same Account into one batch so a parent is locked by only one batch at a time. Sorting by OrderID (A) scatters children of the same parent across batches, causing the very lock collisions you are trying to avoid.

question: To address different compliance requirements, such as general data protection regulation (GDPR), personally identifiable information (PII), of health insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) and others, a SF customer decided to categorize each data element in SF with the following: Data owner Security Level, such as confidential Compliance types such as GDPR, PII, HIPAA A compliance audit would require SF admins to generate reports to manage compliance. What should a data architect recommend to address this requirement?
optionA: Use metadata API, to extract field attribute information and use the extract to classify and build reports
optionB: Use field metadata attributes for compliance categorization, data owner, and data sensitivity level.
optionC: Create a custom object and field to capture necessary compliance information and build custom reports.
optionD: Build reports for field information, then export the information to classify and report for Audits.
explanation: Data Classification adds native field metadata (Data Owner, Data Sensitivity Level, Compliance Categorization) directly on each field, set declaratively and surfaced in reporting. A custom object (C) duplicates and decouples the classification from the actual field definitions, and Metadata API extracts (A) go stale the moment fields change.

question: UC has one SF org (Org A) and recently acquired a secondary company with its own Salesforce org (Org B). UC has decided to keep the orgs running separately but would like to bidirectionally share opportunities between the orgs in near-real time. Which 3 options should a data architect recommend to share data between Org A and Org B? Choose 3 answers.
optionA: Leverage Heroku Connect and Heroku Postgres to bidirectionally sync Opportunities.
optionB: Install a 3rd party AppExchange tool to handle the data sharing
optionC: Develop an Apex class that pushes opportunity data between orgs daily via the Apex schedule.
optionD: Leverage middleware tools to bidirectionally send Opportunity data across orgs.
optionE: Use Salesforce Connect and the cross-org adapter to visualize Opportunities into external objects
explanation: Heroku Connect with Postgres (A) and a middleware/ESB integration (D) both support genuine near-real-time bidirectional sync. The cross-org Salesforce Connect adapter (E) gives real-time virtualized visibility across orgs without copying data. A scheduled daily Apex push (C) violates the near-real-time requirement. Note: the cross-org adapter is read-only, so 'bidirectional' here means each org can view the other's data, not write to it.

question: A health care provider wishes to use Salesforce to track patient care. The following actions are in Salesforce 1. Payment Providers: Organizations who pay for the care 2 patients. 2. Doctors: They provide care plan for patients and need to support multiple patients, they are provided access to patient information. 3. Patients: They are individuals who need care. A data architect needs to map the actor to Sf objects. What should be the optimal selection by the data architect?
optionA: Patients as Contacts, Payment providers as Accounts, & Doctors as Accounts
optionB: Patients as Person Accounts, Payment providers as Accounts, & Doctors as Contacts
optionC: Patients as Person Accounts, Payment providers as Accounts, & Doctors as Person Account
optionD: Patients as Accounts, Payment providers as Accounts, & Doctors as Person Accounts
explanation: Patients are individuals with no parent org, so Person Accounts fit; payment providers are organizations, so business Accounts fit; doctors are also individuals serving many patients, so Person Accounts let them relate to many patient records via junction objects. Modeling doctors as Contacts (B) forces them under a single parent Account, conflicting with serving multiple patients. Note: because doctors need login access to patient data, a real implementation would typically also provision them as Users, an option not offered here.

question: As part of addressing general data protection regulation (GDPR) requirements, UC plans to implement a data classification policy for all its internal systems that stores customer information including Salesforce. What should a data architect recommend so that UC can easily classify consumer information maintained in Salesforce under both standard and custom objects?
optionA: Use AppExchange products to classify fields based on policy.
optionB: Use data classification metadata fields available in field definition.
optionC: Create a custom picklist field to capture classification of information on customer.
optionD: Build reports for customer information and validate.
explanation: Native Data Classification metadata on each field (Data Sensitivity Level and Compliance Categorization) works uniformly across standard and custom objects with no code or extra objects. A custom picklist (C) only works where you add it and isn't tied to field metadata; AppExchange tools (A) add cost for what the platform provides natively.

question: UC is trying to switch from legacy CRM to Salesforce and wants to keep legacy CRM and Salesforce in place till all the functionality is deployed in Salesforce. The want to keep data in sync b/w Salesforce, legacy CRM and SAP. What is the recommendation.
optionA: Integrate legacy CRM to Salesforce and keep data in sync till new functionality is in place
optionB: Do not integrate legacy CRM to Salesforce, but integrate Salesforce to SAP
optionC: Integrate SAP with Salesforce, SAP to legacy CRM but not legacy CRM to Salesforce
optionD: Suggest MDM solution and link MDM to Salesforce and SAP
explanation: Routing both CRMs through SAP (C) keeps SAP as the hub/system of record and avoids a brittle CRM-to-CRM link that breeds duplication and circular updates. An MDM solution (D) provides a governed golden record and survivorship across all three systems. A direct legacy-CRM-to-Salesforce integration (A) creates exactly the two-way sync conflicts the hub-and-spoke pattern prevents.

question: Universal Containers has two systems. Salesforce and an on-premise ERP system. An architect has been tasked with copying Opportunity records to the ERP once they reach a Closed/Won Stage. The Opportunity record in the ERP system will be read-only for all fields copied in from Salesforce. What is the optimal real-time approach that achieves this solution?
optionA: Implement a Master Data Management system to determine system of record.
optionB: Implement a workflow rule that sends Opportunity data through Outbound Messaging.
optionC: Have the ERP poll Salesforce nightly and bring in the desired Opportunities.
optionD: Implement an hourly integration to send Salesforce Opportunities to the ERP system.
explanation: Outbound Messaging fires a near-real-time SOAP callout to the ERP exactly when Stage = Closed/Won, declaratively and with no code. Nightly polling (C) and hourly batch (D) are not real-time, and MDM (A) governs survivorship, not event-driven one-way push. Note: workflow rules are being retired in favor of record-triggered Flows, but the Outbound Message action remains the mechanism.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is facing data quality issues where Sales Reps are creating duplicate customer accounts, contacts, and leads. UC wants to fix this issue immediately by prompting users about a record that possibly exists in Salesforce. UC wants a report regarding duplicate records. What would be the recommended approach to help UC start immediately?
optionA: Create an after insert and update trigger on the account, contact and lead, and send an error if a duplicate is found using a custom matching criteria.
optionB: Create a duplicate rule for account, lead, and contact, use standard matching rules for these objects, and set the action to report and alert for both creates and edits.
optionC: Create a duplicate rule for account, lead, and contact, use standard matching rules for these objects, and set the action to block for both creates and edits.
optionD: Create a before insert and update trigger on account, contact, and lead, and send an error if a duplicate is found using a custom matching criteria.
explanation: Native Duplicate Management with the Alert action prompts users about likely matches at save time, and Report populates the Duplicate Record Set/Items objects for reporting, both requirements, declaratively. Block (C) prevents saving rather than merely prompting, contradicting 'prompt the user'; custom triggers (A, D) re-implement what the platform already provides.

question: Which two best practices should be followed when using SOSL for searching?
optionA: Use searches against single Objects for greater speed and accuracy.
optionB: Keep searches specific and avoid wildcards where possible.
optionC: Use SOSL option to ignore custom indexes as search fields are pre-indexed.
optionD: Use Find in "ALL FIELDS" for faster searches.
explanation: Narrowing a SOSL search to the specific objects/fields needed reduces the search surface and improves speed and relevance (A), and keeping terms specific while avoiding leading wildcards lets the search index work (B). Searching IN ALL FIELDS (D) broadens scope and slows the search, so it is not a best practice; there is no option to tell SOSL to 'ignore custom indexes' (C). Corrected from the source key.

question: Universal Container (US) is replacing a home-grown CRM solution with Salesforce, UC has decided to migrate operational (Open and active) records to Salesforce, while keeping historical records in legacy system, UC would like historical records to be available in Salesforce on an as needed basis. Which solution should a data architect recommend to meet business requirement?
optionA: Leverage real-time integration to pull records into Salesforce.
optionB: Bring all data Salesforce, and delete it after a year.
optionC: Leverage mashup to display historical records in Salesforce.
optionD: Build a custom solution to go to the legacy system and display records.
explanation: A mashup (or Salesforce Connect external objects) surfaces historical legacy data inside Salesforce on demand without copying it, satisfying 'as-needed access' while keeping storage and performance lean. Pulling everything in (B) defeats the purpose and consumes storage; a fully custom build (D) is unnecessary effort versus a virtualized display pattern.

question: A manager at Cloud Kicks is importing Leads into Salesforce and needs to avoid creating duplicate records. Which two approaches should the manager take to achieve this goal? (Choose two.)
optionA: Acquire an AppExchange Lead de-duplication application.
optionB: Implement Salesforce Matching and Duplicate Rules.
optionC: Run the Salesforce Lead Mass de-duplication tool.
optionD: Create a Workflow Rule to check for duplicate records.
explanation: Native Matching and Duplicate Rules (B) detect/prevent duplicate Leads at insert time during the import, and an AppExchange de-duplication app (A) adds fuzzy matching and bulk merge beyond standard rules. There is no native 'Lead Mass de-duplication tool' (C), and Workflow Rules (D) cannot block or merge duplicates.

question: Universal Containers (UC) loads bulk leads and campaigns from third-party lead aggregators on a weekly and monthly basis. The expected lead record volume is 500K records per week, and the expected campaign records volume is 10K campaigns per week. After the upload, Lead records are shared with various sales agents via sharing rules and added as Campaign members via Apex triggers on Lead creation. UC agents work on leads for 6 months, but want to keep the records in the system for at least 1 year for reference. Compliance requires them to be stored for a minimum of 3 years. After that, data can be deleted. What statement is true with respect to a data archiving strategy for UC?
optionA: UC can store long-term lead records in custom storage objects to avoid counting against storage limits.
optionB: UC can leverage the Salesforce Data Backup and Recovery feature for data archival needs.
optionC: UC can leverage recycle bin capability, which guarantees record storage for 15 days after deletion.
optionD: UC can leverage a "tier"-based approach to classify the record storage need.
explanation: A tiered retention model (active 6 months, reference up to 1 year, compliance to 3 years, then purge) lets UC apply the right storage/archival mechanism to each tier. There is no 'custom storage object' exempt from storage limits (A); Backup and Recovery (B) is for restore, not retention policy; the recycle bin (C) is a short-lived soft-delete buffer, not an archive.

question: Universal Containers (UC) needs to move millions of records from an external enterprise resource planning (ERP) system into Salesforce. What should a data architect recommend to be done while using the Bulk API in serial mode instead of parallel mode?
optionA: Placing 20 batches on the queue for upsert jobs.
optionB: Inserting 1 million orders distributed across a variety of accounts with potential lock exceptions.
optionC: Leveraging a controlled feed load with 10 batches per job.
optionD: Inserting 1 million orders distributed across a variety of accounts with lock exceptions eliminated and managed.
explanation: Serial mode processes one batch at a time, so it is the right choice precisely when parent-record contention (many orders rolling up to shared accounts) would otherwise cause lock exceptions in parallel mode (B). Option D assumes locks are already eliminated, which removes the very reason to drop to the slower serial mode.

question: Northern Trail Outfitters (NTO) wants to start a loyalty program to reward repeat customers. The program will track every item a customer has bought and grants them points for discounts. The following conditions will exist upon implementation: Data will be used to drive marketing and product development initiatives. NTO estimates that the program will generate 100 million rows of date monthly. NTO will use Salesforce's Einstein Analytics and Discovery to leverage their data and make business and marketing decisions. What should the Data Architect do to store, collect, and use the reward program data?
optionA: Create a custom big object in Salesforce which will be used to capture the Reward Program data for consumption by Einstein.
optionB: Have Einstein connect to the point of sales system to capture the Reward Program data.
optionC: Create a big object in Einstein Analytics to capture the Loyalty Program data.
optionD: Create a custom object in Salesforce that will be used to capture the Reward Program data.
explanation: At 100M rows/month, a custom big object stores the data at scale without consuming standard storage or degrading transactional performance, and CRM Analytics can ingest big object data for reporting. A standard custom object (D) would blow past practical storage/performance limits, and big objects are a platform construct, not created 'in Einstein Analytics' (C).

question: Cloud Kicks is launching a Partner Community, which will allow users to register shipment requests that are then processed by Cloud Kicks employees. Shipment requests contain header information, and then a list of no more than 5 items being shipped. First, Cloud Kicks will introduce its community to 6,000 customers in North America, and then to 24,000 customers worldwide within the next two years. Cloud Kicks expects 12 shipment requests per week per customer, on average, and wants customers to be able to view up to three years of shipment requests and use Salesforce reports. What is the recommended solution for the Cloud Kicks Data Architect to address the requirements?
optionA: Create an external custom object to track shipment requests and a child external object to track shipment items. External objects are stored off-platform in Heroku's Postgres database.
optionB: Create an external custom object to track shipment requests with five lookup custom fields for each item being shipped. External objects are stored off-platform in Heroku's Postgres database.
optionC: Create a custom object to track shipment requests and a child custom object to track shipment items. Implement an archiving process that moves data off-platform after three years.
optionD: Create a custom object to track shipment requests with five lookup custom fields for each item being shipped Implement an archiving process that moves data off-platform after three years.
explanation: Standard custom objects support native Salesforce reporting (a stated requirement), and a header/child model handles the variable item list cleanly; an archiving job offloads data past the 3-year window to control LDV. External objects (A, B) cannot be reported on natively, and flattening five items into lookup fields (B, D) is rigid and breaks normalization.

question: Cloud Kicks has the following requirements: * Their Shipment custom object must always relate to a Product, a Sender, and a Receiver (all separate custom objects). * If a Shipment is currently associated with a Product, Sender, or Receiver, deletion of those records should not be allowed. * Each custom object must have separate sharing models. What should an Architect do to fulfill these requirements?
optionA: Associate the Shipment to each parent record by using a VLOOKUP formula field.
optionB: Create a required Lookup relationship to each of the three parent records.
optionC: Create a Master-Detail relationship to each of the three parent records.
optionD: Create two Master-Detail and one Lookup relationship to the parent records.
explanation: A required Lookup configured with 'Don't allow deletion of the lookup record that's part of a lookup relationship' both enforces the relationship and blocks parent deletion, while leaving each object its own OWD/sharing model. Master-Detail (C, D) forces the detail to inherit the master's sharing (violating separate sharing), and an object supports at most two master-detail relationships, which three parents would exceed.

question: NTO has multiple systems across its enterprise landscape including Salesforce, with disparate version the customer records. In Salesforce, the customer is represented by the contact object. NTO utilizes an MDM solution with these attributes: 1.The MDM solution keeps track of customer master with a master key. 2.The master key is a map to the record ID's from each external system that customer data is stored within. 3.The MDM solution provides de-duplication features, so it acts as the single source of truth. How should a data architect implement the storage of master key within Salesforce?
optionA: Store the master key in Heroku postgres and use Heroku connect for synchronization.
optionB: Create a custom object to store the master key with a lookup field to contact.
optionC: Create an external object to store the master key with a lookup field to contact.
optionD: Store the master key on the contact object as an external ID (Field for referential imports)
explanation: An External ID field on Contact lets the MDM master key act as a stable, indexed cross-system correlation key and enables upsert-by-external-ID during integrations. A separate custom (B) or external (C) object adds a join and complicates matching, and storing the key only in Heroku (A) keeps it off the Contact records that must be matched on it.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has an open sharing model for its Salesforce users to allow all its Salesforce internal users to edit all contacts, regardless of who owns the contact. However, UC management wants to allow only the owner of a contact record to delete that contact. If a user does not own the contact, then the user should not be allowed to delete the record. How should the architect approach the project so that the requirements are met?
optionA: Create a "before delete" trigger to check if the current user is not the owner.
optionB: Set the Sharing settings as Public Read Only for the Contact object.
optionC: Set the profile of the users to remove delete permission from the Contact object.
optionD: Create a validation rule on the Contact object to check if the current user is not the owner.
explanation: Delete is governed by OWD/role hierarchy and the object Delete profile permission, none of which expresses 'only the owner may delete' while still allowing everyone to edit, so a before-delete trigger comparing UserInfo.getUserId() to OwnerId and calling addError() is the correct enforcement point. Removing Delete from the profile (C) blocks owners too, and validation rules (D) do not fire on delete.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has a complex system landscape and is implementing a data governance program for the first time Which two first steps would be appropriate for UC to initiate an assessment of data architecture? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Engage with IT program managers to assess current velocity of projects in the pipeline.
optionB: Engage with database administrators to assess current database performance metrics.
optionC: Engage with executive sponsorship to assess enterprise data strategy and goals.
optionD: Engage with business units and IT to assess current operational systems and data models.
explanation: A governance assessment starts top-down with executive sponsorship to set strategy, scope, and authority (C), then maps the current state by engaging business units and IT on existing systems and data models (D). Project velocity (A) and DB performance metrics (B) are delivery/operational concerns that do not establish governance scope or data-landscape understanding.

question: Universal Container (UC) stores 10 million rows of inventory data in a cloud database, As part of creating a connected experience in Salesforce, UC would like to this inventory data to Sales Cloud without a import. UC has asked its data architect to determine if Salesforce Connect is needed. Which three consideration should the data architect make when evaluating the need for Salesforce Connect?
optionA: You want real-time access to the latest data, from other systems.
optionB: You have a large amount of data and would like to copy subsets of it into Salesforce.
optionC: You need to expose data via a virtual private connection.
optionD: You have a large amount of data that you don't want to copy into your Salesforce org.
optionE: You need to small amounts of external data at any one time.
explanation: Salesforce Connect fits when you need real-time access to source-of-truth data (A), when the volume is too large to want copied in (D), and when each interaction pulls only small slices on demand (E). If you actually want to copy subsets in (B), an ETL/Bulk API load is appropriate; VPN exposure (C) is an infrastructure detail, not a deciding factor for the OData connector.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is expecting to have nearly 5 million shipments records in its Salesforce org. Each shipment record has up to 10 child shipment item records. The Shipment custom object has an Organization-wide Default (OWD) sharing model set to Private and the Shipment Item custom object has a Master-Detail relationship to Shipment. There are 25 sharing rules set on the Shipment custom object, which allow shipment records to be shared to each of UC's 25 business areas around the globe. These sharing rules use public groups, one for each business area plus a number of groups for management and support roles. UC has a high turnover of Sales Reps and often needs to move Sales Reps between business areas in order to meet local demand. What feature would ensure that performance, when moving Sales Reps between regions, remains adequate while meeting existing requirements?
optionA: Implement data archiving for old Shipment records.
optionB: Contact Salesforce to create Skinny tables on Shipment.
optionC: Configure shipment OWD to Public Read/Write.
optionD: Contact Salesforce to enable Defer Sharing Rules
explanation: Moving reps between groups triggers sharing-rule recalculation across 5M Shipments and 50M Shipment Items; Deferred Sharing Recalculation lets admins batch membership changes and run one recalculation, avoiding prolonged locks and slowdowns. Archiving (A) and skinny tables (B) help query/report performance but not membership-change recalculation, and Public Read/Write (C) violates the required Private model.

question: Universal Containers is planning out their archiving and purging plans going forward for their custom objects Topic__c and Comment__c. Several options are being considered, including analytics snapshots, offsite storage, scheduled purges, etc. Which three questions should be considered when designing an appropriate archiving strategy?
optionA: How many fields are defined on the custom objects that need to be archived?
optionB: Which profiles and users currently have access to these custom object records?
optionC: If reporting is necessary, can the information be aggregated into fewer, summary records?
optionD: Will the data being archived need to be reported on or accessed in any way in the future?
optionE: Are there any regulatory restrictions that will influence the archiving and purging plans?
explanation: Archiving design hinges on whether reporting can use aggregated summaries (C), whether/how the data must still be accessed later (D, which dictates on- vs off-platform and virtualization), and regulatory retention constraints (E). Field count (A) and current record-level access (B) do not materially shape the archive/purge strategy.

question: A casino is implementing Salesforce and is planning to build a customer 360 view for a customer who visits its resorts. The casino currently maintained the following systems that records customer activity: 1.Point of sales system: All purchases for a customer. 2.Salesforce: All customer service activity and sales activity for a customer. 3.Mobile app: All bookings, preferences and browser activity for a customer. 4.Marketing: All email, SMS and social campaigns for a customer. Customer service agents using Salesforce would like to view the activities from all system to provide supports to customers. The information has to be current and real time. What strategy should the data architect implement to satisfy this requirement?
optionA: Explore external data sources in Salesforce to build 360 view of customer.
optionB: Use a customer data mart to view the 360 view of customer.
optionC: Migrate customer activities from all 4 systems into Salesforce.
optionD: Periodically upload summary information in Salesforce to build 360 view.
explanation: Salesforce Connect virtualizes the four external systems as external objects so agents see current, real-time activity inside Salesforce without copying or synchronizing it. Migrating everything (C) creates storage/quality problems and is not real-time at the moment of service; periodic summary uploads (D) are neither current nor detailed enough for live support.

question: Universal Containers has a requirement to store more than 100 million records in Salesforce and needs to create a custom big object to support this business requirement. Which two tools should a data architect use to build custom object?
optionA: Use DX to create big object.
optionB: Use Metadata API to create big object.
optionC: Go to Big Object in setup select new to create big object.
optionD: Go to Object Manager in setup and select new to create big object.
explanation: Big objects are defined either declaratively via the Big Objects node in Setup (C) or programmatically by deploying the object/index definition through the Metadata API (B). The standard Object Manager 'New' flow (D) creates standard/custom objects, not big objects; while DX deploys metadata, the creation mechanism it carries is still the Metadata API definition (A).

question: Universal Containers has 30 million case records. The Case object has 80 fields. Agents are reporting reports in the Salesforce org. Which solution should a data architect recommend to improve reporting performance?
optionA: Create a custom object to store aggregate data and run reports.
optionB: Contact Salesforce support to enable skinny table for cases.
optionC: Move data off of the platform and run reporting outside Salesforce, and give access to reports.
optionD: Build reports using custom Lightning components.
explanation: A skinny table is a Salesforce-maintained copy containing the most frequently used Case fields, kept in sync and excluding soft-deleted rows, which removes expensive joins and speeds reporting on a wide (80-field), high-volume (30M) object. Moving data off-platform (C) is a much larger architectural change for an on-platform report problem, and aggregate custom objects (A) require building/maintaining rollups.

question: Cloud Kicks has the following requirements: - Data needs to be sent from Salesforce to an external system to generate invoices from their Order Management System (OMS). - A Salesforce administrator must be able to customize which fields will be sent to the external system without changing code. What are two approaches for fulfilling these requirements? (Choose two.)
optionA: A Set<SObjectField> to determine which fields to send in an HTTP callout.
optionB: An Outbound Message to determine which fields to send to the OMS.
optionC: A Field Set that determines which fields to send in an HTTP callout.
optionD: Enable the field-level security permissions for the fields to send.
explanation: Outbound Messages let an admin pick the fields to transmit entirely declaratively (B), and a Field Set lets an admin add/remove fields that Apex iterates over in the callout without code changes (C). A hard-coded Set<SObjectField> (A) requires editing Apex, and field-level security (D) governs visibility, not which fields an integration sends.

question: Universal Containers has a large volume of Contact data going into Salesforce. There are 100,000 existing contact records. 200,000 new contacts will be loaded. The Contact object has an external ID field that is unique and must be populated for all existing records. What should the architect recommend to reduce data load processing time?
optionA: Load Contact records together using the Streaming API via the Upsert operation.
optionB: Delete all existing records, and then load all records together via the Insert operation.
optionC: Load all records via the Upsert operation to determine new records vs. existing records.
optionD: Load new records via the Insert operation and existing records via the Update operation.
explanation: Splitting into a pure insert for the 200K new records and an update for the 100K existing ones avoids the implicit external-ID match-and-decide work that upsert performs on every row, which is fastest when you already know which set is which. Upsert (C) is simpler but slower because each record incurs a matching lookup; the Streaming API (A) is for event delivery, not bulk loads.

question: Northern Trail Outfitters needs to implement an archive solution for Salesforce dat a. This archive solution needs to help NTO do the following: 1. Remove outdated Information not required on a day-to-day basis. 2. Improve Salesforce performance. Which solution should be used to meet these requirements?
optionA: Identify a location to store archived data and use scheduled batch jobs to migrate and purge the aged data on a nightly basis,
optionB: Identify a location to store archived data, and move data to the location using a time-based workflow.
optionC: Use a formula field that shows true when a record reaches a defined age and use that field to run a report and export a report into SharePoint.
optionD: Create a full copy sandbox, and use it as a source for retaining archived data.
explanation: Scheduled batch jobs can both move aged records to an external store and hard-delete them from Salesforce nightly, removing clutter and freeing storage to improve performance. Time-based workflows (B) cannot move data off-platform or delete records; report exports to SharePoint (C) purge nothing; a sandbox (D) is a dev/test copy, not an archive.

question: Universal Container is Implementing Salesforce and needs to migrate data from two legacy systems. UC would like to clean and duplicate data before migrate to Salesforce. Which solution should a data architect recommend a clean migration?
optionA: Define external IDs for an object, migrate second database to first database, and load into Salesforce.
optionB: Define duplicate rules in Salesforce, and load data into Salesforce from both databases.
optionC: Set up staging data base, and define external IDs to merge, clean duplicate data, and load into Salesforce.
optionD: Define external IDs for an object, Insert data from one database, and use upsert for a second database
explanation: A staging database is the right place to consolidate both sources, key them on External IDs, and run cleansing/merge logic so only deduplicated, validated data is loaded, keeping the org clean. Relying on Salesforce duplicate rules (B) or upsert (D) catches some collisions but performs cleansing inside the target org rather than before load, leaving partial duplicates and dirty data.

question: All accounts and opportunities are created in Salesforce. Salesforce is integrated with three systems: * An ERP system feeds order data into Salesforce and updates both Account and Opportunity records. * An accounting system feeds invoice data into Salesforce and updates both Account and Opportunity records. * A commission system feeds commission data into Salesforce and updates both Account and Opportunity records. How should the architect determine which of these systems is the system of record?
optionA: Account and opportunity data originates in Salesforce, and therefore Salesforce is the system of record.
optionB: Whatever system updates the attribute or object should be the system of record for that field or object.
optionC: Whatever integration data flow runs last will, by default, determine which system is the system of record.
optionD: Data flows should be reviewed with the business users to determine the system of record per object or field.
explanation: System of record is a business decision made per object/field, established by reviewing each data flow with business stakeholders who understand authority and data quality (D). Declaring Salesforce SOR because data originates there (A) ignores authoritative downstream updates; 'last writer wins' (C) is accidental, not governed; and 'whoever updates it' (B) breaks down when multiple systems write the same field.

question: Universal Containers (UC) maintains a collection of several million Account records that represent business in the United States. As a logistics company, this list is one of the most valuable and important components of UC's business, and the accuracy of shipping addresses is paramount. Recently it has been noticed that too many of the addresses of these businesses are inaccurate, or the businesses don't exist. Which two scalable strategies should UC consider to improve the quality of their Account addresses?
optionA: Contact each business on the list and ask them to review and update their address information.
optionB: Build a team of employees that validate Accounts by searching the web and making phone calls.
optionC: Integrate with a third-party database or services for address validation and enrichment.
optionD: Leverage Data.com Clean to clean up Account address fields with the D&B database.
explanation: Integrating a third-party address validation/enrichment service (C) scales to millions of records by automatically verifying and standardizing addresses against authoritative reference data. Manual outreach (A) and a human validation team (B) do not scale. Note: Data.com Clean (D) was retired by Salesforce in early 2021, so a current implementation would use a third-party data service or Data Cloud enrichment, verify against the present exam outline.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is implementing a formal, cross-business -unit data governance program As part of the program, UC will implement a team to make decisions on enterprise -wide data governance. Which two roles are appropriate as members of this team? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Analytics/BI Owners
optionB: Data Domain Stewards
optionC: Salesforce Administrators
optionD: Operational Data Users
explanation: A governance decision-making body is staffed by roles with definitional authority: Data Domain Stewards (B) own quality standards and rules for their domains, and Analytics/BI Owners (A) define enterprise reporting requirements and metrics. Administrators (C) and operational users (D) execute and consume rather than set enterprise governance policy.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has users complaining about reports timing out or simply taking too long to run What two actions should the data architect recommend to improve the reporting experience? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Index key fields used in report criteria.
optionB: Enable Divisions for large data objects.
optionC: Create one skinny table per report.
optionD: Share each report with fewer users.
explanation: Indexing the fields used in report filter criteria (A) makes the query selective and avoids full table scans, and a skinny table (C) eliminates joins and excludes soft-deleted rows to speed wide/high-volume reports. Divisions (B) partition data but are a coarse, rarely-used construct, and reducing the audience (D) does not change query execution time.

question: Northern Trail Outfitters Is planning to build a consent form to record customer authorization for marketing purposes. What should a data architect recommend to fulfill this requirement?
optionA: Use custom fields to capture the authorization details.
optionB: Create a custom object to maintain the authorization.
optionC: Utilize the Authorization Form Consent object to capture the consent.
optionD: Use AppExchange solution to address the requirement.
explanation: The standard Authorization Form Consent object (part of the Individual/Consent data model) records what a customer agreed to, when, and through which channel/source, supporting privacy/GDPR/marketing consent tracking out of the box. Custom fields or objects (A, B) reinvent this, and an AppExchange package (D) is unnecessary cost for native functionality.

question: Northern Trail Outfitters has these simple requirements for a data export process: File format should be in CSV. Process should be scheduled and run once per week. The expert should be configurable through the Salesforce UI. Which tool should a data architect leverage to accomplish these requirements?
optionA: Bulk API
optionB: Data Export Service (scheduled export)
optionC: Third-party ETL tool
optionD: Data loader
explanation: The built-in Data Export Service lets an admin schedule a weekly CSV export entirely through the Salesforce UI with no code or external tooling, exactly the three requirements. The Bulk API (A) requires development, and Data Loader/ETL tools (C, D) can be scheduled only via command-line/cron, not configured through the Salesforce UI.

question: Salesforce is being deployed in Ursa Major Solar's disparate, multi-system ERP environment. Ursa major Solar wants to maintain data synchronization between systems. Which two techniques should be used to achieve this goal? (Choose two.)
optionA: Integrate Salesforce with the ERP environment.
optionB: Utilize workbench to update files within systems.
optionC: Utilize an MDM strategy to outline a single source of truth.
optionD: Build synchronization reports and dashboards.
explanation: Direct integration between Salesforce and the ERP systems (A) moves data to keep systems in sync, and an MDM strategy (C) defines the single source of truth and survivorship so synchronized data stays consistent. Workbench (B) is an ad-hoc admin tool, and reports/dashboards (D) only observe data, they do not synchronize it.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has lead assignment rules to assign leads to owners. Leads not routed by assignment rules are assigned to a dummy user. Sales rep are complaining of high load times and issues with accessing leads assigned to the dummy user. What should a data architect recommend to solve these performance issues?
optionA: Assign dummy user last role in role hierarchy
optionB: Create multiple dummy user and assign leads to them
optionC: Assign dummy user to highest role in role hierarchy
optionD: Periodically delete leads to reduce number of leads
explanation: Funneling unrouted leads to one user creates ownership (account/lead) data skew, where a single user owning tens of thousands of records causes slow sharing recalculation and lock contention. Distributing ownership across multiple dummy users (B) breaks up the skew. Placing the user high in the hierarchy (C) worsens sharing recalculation, and deleting leads (D) is data loss, not a fix.

question: A customer is operating in a highly regulated industry and is planning to implement SF. The customer information maintained in SF, includes the following: Personally, identifiable information (PII) IP restrictions on profiles organized by Geographic location Financial records that need to be private and accessible only by the assigned Sales associate. User should not be allowed to export information from Salesforce. Enterprise security has mandate access to be restricted to users within a specific geography and detail monitoring of user activity. Which 3 Salesforce shield capabilities should a data architect recommend? Choose 3 answers:
optionA: Event monitoring to monitor all user activities
optionB: Restrict access to SF from users outside specific geography
optionC: Prevent Sales users access to customer PII information
optionD: Transaction security policies to prevent export of SF Data.
optionE: Encrypt Sensitive Customer information maintained in SF.
explanation: Shield comprises Event Monitoring, Platform Encryption, and Field Audit Trail; the mandate for detailed monitoring maps to Event Monitoring (A), preventing export maps to a Transaction Security policy (D), and protecting financial/PII data maps to Platform Encryption (E). Geographic restriction (B) is achieved with login-IP ranges/network access, not a Shield capability, and blanket-blocking PII for sales users (C) is a sharing/FLS concern. Corrected from the source key.

question: Universal Container (UC) has accumulated data over years and has never deleted data from its Salesforce org. UC is now exceeding the storage allocations in the org. UC is now looking for option to delete unused from the org. Which three recommendations should a data architect make is order to reduce the number of records from the org? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Use hard delete in Bulk API to permanently delete records from Salesforce.
optionB: Use hard delete in batch Apex to permanently delete records from Salesforce.
optionC: Identify records in objects that have not been modified or used In last 3 years.
optionD: Use Rest API to permanently delete records from the Salesforce org.
optionE: Archive the records in enterprise data warehouse (EDW) before deleting from Salesforce.
explanation: First identify candidates, records untouched for years (C); preserve them by archiving to an EDW before removal (E); then permanently remove them with Bulk API hard delete (A), which bypasses the recycle bin and is most efficient at volume. Batch Apex (B) and REST (D) can delete but are slower/less scalable for mass purges and lack a comparable hard-delete mechanism.

question: What makes Skinny tables fast? Choose three answers.
optionA: They do not include soft-deleted records
optionB: They avoid resource intensive joins
optionC: Their tables are kept in sync with their source tables when the source tables are modified
optionD: They can contain fields from other objects
optionE: They support up to a max of 100 of columns
explanation: Skinny tables are fast because they exclude soft-deleted (recycle bin) records so scans are smaller (A), they hold frequently used fields in one table to avoid joins between base and custom-field tables (B), and Salesforce keeps them automatically synchronized with the source object (C). They are limited to fields from a single object (D), and the 100-column cap (E) is a limit, not a performance driver.

question: DreamHouse Realty has a data model as shown in the image. The Project object has a private sharing model, and it has Roll-Up summary fields to calculate the number of resources assigned to the project, total hours for the project, and the number of work items associated to the project. There will be a large amount of time entry records to be loaded regularly from an external system into Salesforce. What should the Architect consider in this situation?
optionA: Load all data after deferring sharing calculations.
optionB: Calculate summary values instead of Roll-Up by using workflow.
optionC: Calculate summary values instead of Roll-Up by using triggers.
optionD: Load all data using external IDs to link to parent records.
explanation: Loading large volumes of child time-entry records into a privately-shared parent triggers both sharing recalculation and roll-up recomputation per parent, causing locks and slow loads; deferring sharing calculations and loading in bulk first, then recalculating, mitigates this. Replacing roll-ups with workflow (B) or triggers (C) does not help and adds governor-limit risk. Note: the original exhibit was not provided, so this is a best-guess based on the described model.

question: A large Automobile company has implemented SF for its Sales Associates. Leads flow from its website to SF using a batch integration in SF. The Batch job connects the leads to Accounts in SF. Customer visiting their retail stores are also created in SF as Accounts. The company has noticed a large number of duplicate accounts in SF. On analysis, it was found that certain customers could interact with its website and also visit the store. The Sales associates use Global Search to search for customers in Salesforce before they create the customers. Which scalable option should a data Architect choose to implement to avoid duplicates?
optionA: Create duplicate rules in SF to validate duplicates during the account creation process
optionB: Implement a MDM solution to validate the customer information before creating Accounts in SF.
optionC: Build Custom search based on fields on Accounts which can be matched with customer when they visit the store
optionD: Customize Account creation process to search if customer exists before creating an Account.
explanation: Native Duplicate Management with matching and duplicate rules (A) catches matches at both the batch-integration insert and the manual store-created insert without custom code, the scalable low-maintenance choice. An MDM solution (B) is heavier infrastructure than needed here, and custom search/creation logic (C, D) duplicates platform capability with development effort.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is migrating data from legacy system to Salesforce. During data analysis it was discovered that data types of fields being migrated do not match with Salesforce data types. Which solution should a data architect use to ensure successful data migrations?
optionA: Migrate legacy data to a staging database for mapping then leverage an ETL tool to transform the data and load into Salesforce.
optionB: Export legacy data into the staging database and leverage stored procedures to transform data types before loading into Salesforce.
optionC: Migrate the legacy data leveraging an ETL tool to transform data types and load data into Salesforce.
optionD: Export legacy data into CSV files and leverage data loader to load data into Salesforce.
explanation: An ETL tool natively maps and transforms mismatched data types in-flight (plus cleansing, formatting, and loading) without standing up an interim database. Adding a staging database (A) or stored procedures (B) introduces extra infrastructure the ETL layer already provides, and Data Loader (D) only loads CSVs, performing no type transformation.

question: Northern trail Outfitters (NTO) uses Sales Cloud and service Cloud to manage sales and support processes. Some of NTOs team are complaining they see new fields on their page unsure of which values need be input. NTO is concerned about lack of governance in making changes to Salesforce. Which governance measure should a data architect recommend to solve this issue?
optionA: Add description fields to explain why the field is used, and mark the field as required.
optionB: Create and manage a data dictionary and set up a governance process for changes made to common objects.
optionC: Create reports to identify which users are leaving fields blank, and use external data sources to augment the missing data.
optionD: Create validation rules with error messages to explain why the fields is used
explanation: A maintained data dictionary documents each field's purpose and a change-governance process controls who can add fields and why, addressing the root cause of uncontrolled, undocumented metadata changes. Field descriptions/required flags (A) and validation rules (D) treat the symptom on individual fields but do not govern change, and reporting on blanks (C) measures the problem rather than preventing it.

question: Universal Containers (UC) owns a complex Salesforce org with many Apex classes, triggers, and automated processes that will modify records if available. UC has identified that, in its current development state, UC runs change of encountering race condition on the same record. What should a data architect recommend to guarantee that records are not being updated at the same time?
optionA: Embed the keywords FOR UPDATE after SOQL statements.
optionB: Disable classes or triggers that have the potential to obtain the same record.
optionC: Migrate programmatic logic to processes and flows.
optionD: Refactor or optimize classes and trigger for maximum CPU performance.
explanation: Adding FOR UPDATE to a SOQL query takes a row lock on the selected records for the transaction, so a competing transaction must wait, preventing the lost-update race condition. Disabling automation (B) removes functionality rather than coordinating concurrency, moving logic to Flows (C) provides no pessimistic locking, and CPU optimization (D) addresses limits, not concurrency.

question: UC has a legacy client server app that as a relational data base that needs to be migrated to Salesforce. What are the 3 key actions that should be done when data modeling in Salesforce? Choose 3 answers:
optionA: Identify data elements to be persisted in Salesforce.
optionB: Map legacy data to Salesforce objects.
optionC: Map legacy data to Salesforce custom objects.
optionD: Work with legacy application owner to analysis legacy data model.
optionE: Implement legacy data model within Salesforce using custom fields.
explanation: Sound data modeling decides which elements need to live in Salesforce (A), maps those elements to the appropriate Salesforce objects (B), then realizes the model with custom objects/fields (E). Mapping only to custom objects (C) is a narrower subset of B. Note: collaborating with the legacy owner to analyze the source model (D) is a defensible alternative third choice, flag for review against the official outline.

question: UC has millions of Cases and are running out of storage. Some user groups need to have access to historical cases for up to 7 years. Which 2 solutions should a data architect recommend in order to minimize performance and storage issues? Choose 2 answers:
optionA: Export data out of Salesforce and store in Flat files on external system.
optionB: Create a custom object to store case history and run reports on it.
optionC: Leverage on premise data archival and build integration to view archived data.
optionD: Leverage big object to archive case data and lightning components to show archived data.
explanation: On-premise/off-platform archival with an integration to surface the data (C) and big objects with Lightning components to display archived cases (D) both move volume off the transactional store while keeping 7-year data accessible. Flat files (A) make the data hard to query, and a standard custom object (B) still consumes storage and degrades performance at millions of rows.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has three systems: Salesforce, a cloud -based ERP system, and an on-premise Order Management System (OMS). An architect has been tasked with creating a solution that uses Salesforce as the system of record for Leads and the OMS as the system of record for Account and Contacts. UC wants Accounts and Contacts to be able to maintain their names in each system (i.e., "John Doe" in the OMS and "Johnny Doe" in Salesforce), but wants to have a consolidated data store which links referenced records across the systems. What approach should an architect suggest so the requirements are met?
optionA: Have Salesforce poll the OMS nightly and bring in the desired Accounts and Contacts.
optionB: Implement an integration tool to send OMS Accounts and Contacts to Salesforce.
optionC: Implement a Master Data Management strategy to reconcile Leads, Accounts, and Contacts.
optionD: Use the Streaming API to send Account and Contact data from Salesforce to the OMS.
explanation: Only MDM supports the stated requirements: different systems of record per entity, allowed attribute divergence (different names per system), and a consolidated store with cross-references linking the records. Point-to-point sync (A, B) would overwrite the intentionally divergent names and provides no golden-record linkage, and the Streaming API (D) is one-way event delivery, not reconciliation.

question: Universal Containers has millions of rows of data in Salesforce that are being used in reports to evaluate historical trends. Performance has become an issue, as well as data storage limits. Which two strategies should be recommended when talking with stakeholders?
optionA: Use scheduled batch Apex to copy aggregate information into a custom object and delete the original records.
optionB: Combine Analytics Snapshots with a purging plan by reporting on the snapshot data and deleting the original records.
optionC: Use Data Loader to extract data, aggregate it, and write it back to a custom object, then delete the original records.
optionD: Configure the Salesforce Archiving feature to archive older records and remove them from the data storage limits.
explanation: Analytics/Reporting Snapshots (B) is the native feature for capturing aggregated point-in-time data on a schedule for trend reporting, and pairing it with a purge of detail records reclaims storage. Scheduled batch Apex that rolls detail into a summary object and deletes the originals (A) achieves the same on-platform. The external Data Loader round-trip (C) is fragile, and there is no general native 'Salesforce Archiving feature' for arbitrary custom records (D). Corrected from the source key.

question: UC is building a Salesforce application to track contacts and their respective conferences that they have attended with the following requirements: 1.Contacts will be stored in the standard contact object. 2.Conferences will be stored in a custom conference_ c object. 3.Each contact may attend multiple conferences and each conference may be related to multiple contacts. How should a data architect model the relationship between the contact and conference objects?
optionA: Implement a Contact Conference junction object with master detail relationship to both contact and Conference__c
optionB: Create a master detail relationship field on the Contact object.
optionC: Create a master detail relationship field on the Conference object.
optionD: Create a lookup relationship field on contact object.
explanation: A many-to-many relationship requires a junction object with two master-detail relationships (to Contact and to Conference__c), letting each contact attend many conferences and each conference include many contacts while enforcing referential integrity. A single master-detail or lookup (B, C, D) can only model a one-to-many relationship.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is a business that works directly with individual consumers (B2C). They are moving from a current home-grown CRM system to Salesforce. UC has about one million consumer records. What should the architect recommend for optimal use of Salesforce functionality and also to avoid data loading issues?
optionA: Create a Custom Object Individual_Consumer__c to load all individual consumers.
optionB: Load all individual consumers as Account records and avoid using the Contact object.
optionC: Load one Account record and one Contact record for each individual consumer.
optionD: Create one Account and load individual consumers as Contacts linked to that one Account.
explanation: Given the options (no Person Accounts choice), the 'bucket account' pattern, one placeholder Account with consumers loaded as Contacts (D), avoids creating a million Accounts and leverages the standard Contact object. Note: loading ~1M Contacts under a single Account causes severe account data skew (a parent with >10K children) and would, in a real implementation, be solved with Person Accounts instead, flag this as a flawed/limited question.

question: Universal Containers (UC) is in the process of selling half of its company. As part of this split, UC's main Salesforce org will be divided into two org:org A and org B, UC has delivered these requirements to its data architect 1. The data model for Org B will drastically change with different objects, fields, and picklist values. 2. Three million records will need to be migrated from org A to org B for compliance reasons. 3. The migrate will need occur within the next two month, prior to be split. Which migrate strategy should a data architect use to successfully migrate the date?
optionA: use as ETL tool to orchestrate the migration.
optionB: Use Data Loader for export and Data Import Wizard for import
optionC: Write a script to use the Bulk API
optionD: Use the Salesforce CLI to query, export, and import
explanation: An ETL tool handles the drastic model/field/picklist remapping, transforms values between orgs, and moves 3M records within the timeline through repeatable jobs. Data Import Wizard (B) is capped well below 3M records and cannot do complex transformation; hand-rolled Bulk API scripts (C) and the CLI (D) are far more effort for a transforming org-to-org migration.

question: Get Cloudy Consulting uses an invoicing system that has specific requirements. One requirement is that attachments associated with the Invoice__c custom object be classified by Types (i.e., ""Purchase Order"", ""Receipt"", etc.) so that reporting can be performed on invoices showing the number of attachments grouped by Type. What should an Architect do to categorize the attachments to fulfill these requirements?
optionA: Add additional options to the standard ContentType picklist field for the Attachment object.
optionB: Add a ContentType picklist field to the Attachment layout and create additional picklist options.
optionC: Create a custom picklist field for the Type on the standard Attachment object with the values.
optionD: Create a custom object related to the Invoice object with a picklist field for the Type.
explanation: The classic Attachment object does not support custom fields, so the supported pattern is a custom object related to Invoice__c that stores each attachment reference plus a Type picklist, enabling grouped reporting. Options A-C all assume you can add or extend picklists on the Attachment object, which is not possible.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has adopted Salesforce as its primary sales automated tool. UC has 100,00 customers with a growth rate of 10% a year, UC uses an on-premise web-based billing and invoice system that generates over 1 million invoices a year supporting a monthly billing cycle. The UC sales team needs to be able to pull a customer record and view their account status, Invoice history, and opportunities without navigating outside of Salesforce. What should a data architect use to provide the sales team with the required functionality?
optionA: Create a custom object and migrate the last 12 months of Invoice data into Salesforce so it can be displayed on the Account layout.
optionB: Write an Apex callout and populate a related list to display on the account record.
optionC: Create a mashup page that will present the billing system records within Salesforce.
optionD: Create a visual force tab with the billing system encapsulated within an iframe.
explanation: A mashup surfaces the high-volume billing/invoice data live from the on-premise system inside the customer record without copying 1M+ invoices/year into Salesforce, giving sales the consolidated view while avoiding storage cost and stale data. Migrating invoices in (A) consumes storage and goes stale, and an iframe tab (D) does not present the data in the context of the customer record.

question: Universal Containers (UC) uses Salesforce for tracking opportunities (Opportunity). UC uses an internal ERP system for tracking deliveries and invoicing. The ERP system supports SOAP API and OData for bi-directional integration between Salesforce and the ERP system. UC has about one million opportunities. For each opportunity, UC sends 12 invoices, one per month. UC sales reps have requirements to view current invoice status and invoice amount from the opportunity page. When creating an object to model invoices, what should the architect recommend, considering performance and data storage space?
optionA: Use Streaming API to get the current status from the ERP and display on the Opportunity page.
optionB: Create an external object Invoice__x with a Lookup relationship with Opportunity.
optionC: Create a custom object Invoice__c with a master-detail relationship with Opportunity.
optionD: Create a custom object Invoice__c with a Lookup relationship with Opportunity.
explanation: With 12M invoices that live in the ERP and only need to be viewed, an external object (Invoice__x) via OData keeps the data off-platform, zero storage cost and always current, while still letting reps see status and amount on the Opportunity through an external/indirect lookup. Custom objects (C, D) would store 12M rows in Salesforce, and the Streaming API (A) is for event delivery, not displaying invoice history.

question: A customer wants to maintain geographic location information including latitude and longitude in a custom object. What would a data architect recommend to satisfy this requirement?
optionA: Create formula fields with geolocation function for this requirement.
optionB: Create custom fields to maintain latitude and longitude information
optionC: Create a geolocation custom field to maintain this requirement
optionD: Recommend app exchange packages to support this requirement.
explanation: The Geolocation compound field stores latitude and longitude together and supports DISTANCE/GEOLOCATION functions and proximity queries natively. Separate plain number fields (B) lose the compound behavior, a formula field (A) cannot store user-entered coordinates, and an AppExchange package (D) is unnecessary for a built-in field type.

question: Universal containers is implementing Salesforce lead management. UC Procure lead data from multiple sources and would like to make sure lead data as company profile and location information. Which solution should a data architect recommend to make sure lead data has both profile and location information? Option
optionA: Ask sales people to search for populating company profile and location data
optionB: Run reports to identify records which does not have company profile and location data
optionC: Leverage external data providers populate company profile and location data
optionD: Export data out of Salesforce and send to another team to populate company profile and location data
explanation: External data providers (data enrichment services) automatically append authoritative company profile and location attributes to leads at scale, ensuring completeness regardless of source. Manual lookup by reps (A) and hand-off to another team (D) do not scale and are error-prone, and reporting on blanks (B) only identifies the gap.

question: Universal Containers has a legacy system that captures Conferences and Venues. These Conferences can occur at any Venue. They create hundreds of thousands of Conferences per year. Historically, they have only used 20 Venues. Which two things should the data architect consider when denormalizing this data model into a single Conference object with a Venue picklist? Choose 2 answers
optionA: Limitations on master-detail relationships.
optionB: Org data storage limitations.
optionC: Bulk API limitations on picklist fields.
optionD: Standard list view in-line editing.
explanation: Collapsing Venue into a picklist raises picklist-specific considerations: the Bulk API restricts how new/unrestricted picklist values are handled during high-volume loads (C), and standard list-view inline editing of a picklist can introduce unvalidated values without governance (D). Master-detail limits (A) and storage (B) are not specific to the picklist denormalization decision. Note: this is a poorly constructed source question, flag for review.

question: Universal Containers (CU) is in the process of implementing an enterprise data warehouse (EDW). UC needs to extract 100 million records from Salesforce for migration to the EDW. What data extraction strategy should a data architect use for maximum performance?
optionA: Install a third-party AppExchange tool.
optionB: Call the REST API in successive queries.
optionC: Utilize PK Chunking with the Bulk API.
optionD: Use the Bulk API in parallel mode.
explanation: PK Chunking splits a massive extract into sequential batches by record-ID ranges, letting the Bulk API process each chunk efficiently and avoiding the query timeouts that occur on full table scans of 100M rows. Plain Bulk API parallel mode (D) and successive REST queries (B) still risk timeouts at that volume, and an AppExchange tool (A) adds cost without changing the underlying limits.

question: Due to security requirements, Universal Containers needs to capture specific user actions, such as login, logout, file attachment download, package install, etc. What is the recommended approach for defining a solution for this requirement?
optionA: Use a field audit trail to capture field changes.
optionB: Use a custom object and trigger to capture changes.
optionC: Use Event Monitoring to capture these changes.
optionD: Use a third-party AppExchange app to capture changes.
explanation: Event Monitoring exposes login, logout, download, API, and package-install events through Event Log Files (and real-time event objects), purpose-built for this audit requirement. Field Audit Trail (A) tracks field value history, not user actions; custom triggers (B) cannot capture login/logout/download events; an AppExchange app (D) is unnecessary given the native capability.

question: Universals Containers' system administrators have been complaining that they are not able to make changes to its users' record, including moving them to new territories without getting "unable to lock row" errors. This is causing the system admins to spend hours updating user records every day. What should the data architect do to prevent the error?
optionA: Reduce number of users updated concurrently.
optionB: Enable granular locking.
optionC: Analyze Splunk query to spot offending records.
optionD: Increase CPU for the Salesforce org.
explanation: Granular locking changes how Salesforce locks group-membership/role structures so concurrent membership updates and sharing recalculations no longer take a single broad lock, eliminating the 'unable to lock row' contention during user/territory changes. Reducing concurrency (A) is a workaround that hurts throughput, and CPU/Splunk (C, D) do not address row-lock semantics.

question: Universal Containers (UC) has a requirement to create an Account plan object that is related to the Account object. Each Account plan needs to have an Account object, but the accessibility requirement of the Account plan is different from the Account object. What should an Architect recommend?
optionA: Create a custom account plan object as detail with Account as mater in a master-detail relationship.
optionB: Create a custom account plan object as detail with Account as master with additional sharing rules to allow access.
optionC: Create an account plan object with a lookup relations to Account without any validation rules to enforce the Account association.
optionD: Create an account plan object with a lookup relationship to Account with validation rules to enforce the Account association.
explanation: A lookup relationship lets Account Plan keep its own OWD/sharing model (the differing accessibility requirement), and a validation rule enforcing a non-null Account makes the relationship effectively required. Master-detail (A, B) forces the plan to inherit the Account's sharing, violating the accessibility difference, and a lookup without enforcement (C) fails the 'each plan must have an Account' rule.

question: Universal Containers (UC) provides shipping services to its customers. They use Opportunities to track customer shipments. At any given time, shipping status can be one of the 10 values. UC has 200,000 Opportunity records. When creating a new field to track shipping status on opportunity, what should the architect do to improve data quality and avoid data skew?
optionA: Create a picklist field, values sorted alphabetically.
optionB: Create a Master-Detail to custom object ShippingStatus__c.
optionC: Create a Lookup to custom object ShippingStatus__c.
optionD: Create a text field and make it an external ID.
explanation: A restricted picklist enforces the 10 valid values (data quality) and avoids the lookup-driven data skew that would occur if 200,000 Opportunities all pointed at one of 10 ShippingStatus__c records (~20K children each). A lookup or master-detail (B, C) creates exactly that child-record concentration, and a free-text field (D) provides no value control.

question: UC has a roll-up summary field on Account to calculate the count of contacts associated with an account. During the account load, SF is throwing an "Unable to lock a row" error. Which solution should a data architect recommend, to resolve the error?
optionA: Leverage data loader platform API to load data.
optionB: Perform Batch job in parallel mode and reduce Batch size
optionC: Perform Batch job in serial mode and reduce batch size
optionD: Defer roll-up summary fields calculation during data migration.
explanation: Inserting child Contacts forces locks on the parent Account so its roll-up can recompute; running the load in serial mode with smaller batches ensures only one batch touches a given parent at a time, eliminating contention. Parallel mode (B) still allows concurrent batches to hit the same parent, and roll-up summary recalculation cannot simply be deferred during a load (D).

question: US is implementing Salesforce and will be using Salesforce to track customer complaints, provide white papers on products and provide subscription (Fee) - based support. Which license type will US users need to fulfil US's requirements?
optionA: Lightning platform starter license.
optionB: Service cloud license.
optionC: Salesforce license.
optionD: Sales cloud license
explanation: Service Cloud licenses provide case management, knowledge/white-paper, entitlement, and service-contract (fee-based support) capabilities plus standard CRM objects. Sales Cloud (D) lacks the service-specific objects, Platform Starter (A) is limited to one custom app, and 'Salesforce license' (C) is a generic platform license that doesn't specifically target service entitlements.

question: The architect is planning a large data migration for Universal Containers from their legacy CRM system to Salesforce. What three things should the architect consider to optimize performance of the data migration? Choose 3 answers
optionA: Review the time zones of the User loading the data.
optionB: Remove custom indexes on the data being loaded.
optionC: Determine if the legacy system is still in use.
optionD: Defer sharing calculations of the Salesforce Org.
optionE: Deactivate approval processes and workflow rules.
explanation: Dropping custom indexes during the load avoids index-maintenance overhead on every insert (B), deferring sharing calculations prevents per-record sharing recalculation (D), and deactivating approval processes/workflow rules stops automation from firing on each row (E), all of which speed a bulk load. Time-zone review (A) and whether the legacy system is in use (C) do not affect load performance.

question: Universal Containers wants to develop a dashboard in Salesforce that will allow Sales Managers to do data exploration using their mobile device (i.e., drill down into sales-related data) and have the possibility of adding ad-hoc filters while on the move. What is a recommended solution for building data exploration dashboards in Salesforce?
optionA: Create a Dashboard in an external reporting tool, export data to the tool, and add link to the dashboard in Salesforce.
optionB: Create a Dashboard in an external reporting tool, export data to the tool, and embed the dashboard in Salesforce using the Canvas toolkit.
optionC: Create a standard Salesforce Dashboard and connect it to reports with the appropriate filters.
optionD: Create a Dashboard using Analytics Cloud that will allow the user to create ad-hoc lenses and drill down.
explanation: CRM Analytics supports interactive exploration, ad-hoc lenses, drill-down, and on-the-fly filtering on mobile, exactly the experience requested.
